aliases: Lee Kum Kee Premium Oyster Sauce

english: Lee Kum Kee Premium Oyster Sauce

region_of_origin: Guangdong, China; widely used in Singapore and Southeast Asia
food_subgroup: Thickened oyster extract condiment; used as a stir-fry sauce, braising sauce, and vegetable glaze

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for oyster sauce as a branded condiment; closest parent is a generic sauce/condiment class. No code assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Oyster sauce — classified under sauces and condiments in FoodEx2 hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A039T represents the sauces/condiments category; no exact branded oyster sauce code exists in the public FoodEx2 release.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for oyster sauce condiment in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of oyster sauce is 1 tablespoon (approximately 16g), providing about 16 kcal, 512 mg sodium, and 3.5 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Oyster Sauce, Sauce, Oyster — USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 173889); cross-referenced with Lee Kum Kee Singapore product label nutritional panel
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Lee Kum Kee Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central entry for oyster sauce (FDC ID 173889) provides per-100g values consistent with the Lee Kum Kee Premium product label (sodium ~3200 mg/100g, energy ~100 kcal/100g, carbohydrate ~22 g/100g). Values are confirmed against both sources. Fat is confirmed zero by product formulation — oyster sauce contains no added fat and oyster extract contributes negligible fat at this concentration.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sauces and Condiments — analogous estimate based on sugar and modified starch composition; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Lee Kum Kee Premium Oyster Sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~65 (medium) is estimated based on the sauce's carbohydrate composition: approximately 55% sugar (sucrose/glucose, GI ~65) and modified starch (GI ~65–70). Glycemic load per 1 tablespoon (16g) = (65 × 3.52g available carbohydrate) / 100 = ~2.3, which is negligible. Estimation methodology consistent with Brand-Miller et al. (2008) analogous-food approach.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — 3,200 mg sodium per 100g; approximately 512 mg sodium per tablespoon (16g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit oyster sauce to no more than 1 teaspoon per serving and account for it within a daily sodium budget of under 2,000 mg; using 2 tablespoons in a single stir-fry contributes over 1,000 mg sodium from the sauce alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (12 g/100g) but negligible glycemic load at typical condiment serving sizes — GL ~2.3 per tablespoon
type2_diabetes.guidance: At a standard serving of 1 tablespoon (16g), oyster sauce contributes approximately 2g sugar and a glycemic load of ~2.3, which is clinically negligible for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; portion control remains important if multiple tablespoons are used.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content raises cardiovascular risk; fat content is negligible (0g fat, 0g saturated fat per 100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The primary cardiovascular concern with oyster sauce is its very high sodium content (3,200 mg/100g), which contributes to elevated blood pressure and cardiovascular risk; fat and cholesterol content are negligible, but sodium intake from oyster sauce should be carefully managed in individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

shellfish_allergy.key_factor: Contains oyster extract — a shellfish-derived ingredient
shellfish_allergy.guidance: Oyster sauce contains oyster extract and is contraindicated for individuals with shellfish allergies; mushroom-based vegetarian oyster sauce alternatives are available and do not contain shellfish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oyster sauce (蚝油, Háo Yóu) is a thick, dark brown Chinese condiment made from oyster extract, sugar, salt, modified starch, and soy sauce. It was invented accidentally in 1888 by Lee Kum Sheung in Guangdong, China, when oysters being cooked were reduced into a rich, caramelised, intensely savoury sauce. Today it is the most essential Cantonese stir-fry seasoning, used in virtually every restaurant's kai lan with oyster sauce, broccoli stir-fry, and braised dish. Lee Kum Kee Premium Oyster Sauce (green-label) is the dominant brand in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. The sauce has a thick, glossy texture with a deep umami-sweet-savoury flavour profile. Per 100g: approximately 100 kcal, 3g protein, 0g fat, 22g carbohydrate (12g sugar), and 3,200mg sodium. Per standard tablespoon (16g): ~16 kcal, ~512mg sodium, ~3.5g carbohydrate, ~2g sugar. Sodium is the primary nutritional concern — two tablespoons in a stir-fry contributes over 1,000mg sodium. Glycemic load per tablespoon is negligible (~2.3). Not halal (contains oyster extract). Not suitable for shellfish allergy. Dairy-free. Vegetarian oyster sauce alternatives made from mushroom extract are widely available in Singapore and are broadly comparable in macronutrient and sodium profile.
common_user_queries: Lee Kum Kee oyster sauce calories
oyster sauce vs soy sauce sodium

confidence_reason: Nutrient values confirmed against USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 173889) for generic oyster sauce and cross-referenced with Lee Kum Kee Singapore product label nutritional panel. Sodium (3,200 mg/100g), energy (100 kcal/100g), and carbohydrate (22g/100g) are consistent across both sources. GI is an analogous estimate based on carbohydrate composition using the Brand-Miller et al. (2008) methodology.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — oyster sauce contains no added fat and oyster extract contributes negligible fat at condiment concentration; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173889. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, and polyunsaturated_fat_g are all confirmed zero by the same source and product formulation. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per product label. Water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific branded product is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or the Lee Kum Kee Singapore product label as of April 2026. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for Lee Kum Kee Premium Oyster Sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; estimated from carbohydrate composition (sugar and modified starch) using Brand-Miller et al. (2008) methodology. FoodEx2 code A039T is assigned as closest-parent match for sauces/condiments; no exact branded oyster sauce code exists in the public FoodEx2 release. This product is not halal-certified as it contains oyster (shellfish) extract. Vegetarian/vegan alternatives using mushroom extract are available and broadly comparable in macronutrient and sodium profile.
